md: Faced with a proportion like $\dfrac{3}{4} = \dfrac{x}{20}$, students are taught to multiply **diagonally**, $3 \times 20$ and $4 \times x$, set them equal, and solve.
It feels like a trick with no reason behind it. In fact it's a single, ordinary move on an equation. Here's the move.

blocks.title: The claim
blocks.body: Two fractions are equal exactly when their cross-products are equal:  $\;\dfrac{a}{b}=\dfrac{c}{d} \iff ad = bc.$

md: Start with the proportion. The only thing making it awkward is the two denominators, $b$ and $d$. So get rid of them, by multiplying **both sides by $bd$**. (Whatever you do to one side of an equation, you may do to the other; the balance is preserved.)
On the left, the $b$ in the denominator cancels the $b$ in $bd$, leaving $a \times d$. On the right, the $d$ cancels, leaving $c \times b$:
And $cb$ is the same as $bc$. So the proportion is equivalent to:
That's the whole "trick." The diagonal arrows of cross-multiplication are just a picture of *which* terms survive after the denominators cancel, $a$ pairs with $d$, and $b$ pairs with $c$.
Every step is reversible, so it works both directions: equal fractions force equal cross-products, and equal cross-products force equal fractions.

blocks.title: Back to the example
blocks.body: For $\dfrac{3}{4} = \dfrac{x}{20}$: cross-multiplying gives $3 \times 20 = 4 \times x$, so $60 = 4x$, and $x = 15$. One balanced move turned a fraction equation into a plain one.
